title: Kinetic energy of emitted electrons
description: Let's practice some problems to better understand the kinetic energy distribution of the electrons emitted in photoelectric effect.

assessment_items.question_content:
Light of a certain frequency shines on a metal surface resulting in the emission of two electrons: $\text A$ and $\text B$. Electron $\text A$ is more tightly bound than electron $\text B$.

**Compare the kinetic energies of the two electrons after emission.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's say the energy of a photon is $E$. A part of this energy, say $\phi$, is spent in removing the electron from the metal. The remaining appears as the kinetic energy $\left(KE\right)$ of the emitted electron. So, 
$E=\phi+KE$.

To compare the kinetic energies, we need to compare $\phi$ for the two electrons.

##Solution

Electron $\text A$ is more tightly bound, so more energy will be needed to remove it as compared to electron $\text B$ $\Rightarrow \phi_\text A>\phi_\text B$.

Thus, a smaller portion of the photon energy goes to the kinetic energy for electron $\text A$ $\Rightarrow {KE}_\text A<{KE}_\text B$.

The correct answer is:

${KE}_\text A<{KE}_\text B$

assessment_items.question_content:
Light of a certain frequency shines on a metal surface resulting in the emission of two electrons: $\text P$ and $\text Q$. Electron $\text P$ is more loosely bound than electron $\text Q$.

**Compare the kinetic energies of the two electrons after emission.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's say the energy of a photon is $E$. A part of this energy, say $\phi$, is spent in removing the electron from the metal. The remaining appears as the kinetic energy $\left(KE\right)$ of the emitted electron. So, 
$E=\phi+KE$.

To compare the kinetic energies, we need to compare $\phi$ for the two electrons.

##Solution

Electron $\text P$ is more loosely bound, so less energy will be needed to remove it as compared to electron $\text Q$ $\Rightarrow \phi_\text P<\phi_\text Q$.

Thus, a larger portion of the photon energy goes to the kinetic energy for electron $\text P$ $\Rightarrow {KE}_\text P>{KE}_\text Q$.

The correct answer is:


${KE}_\text P>{KE}_\text Q$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two metal surfaces $\text P$ and $\text Q$ having work functions $\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text P$ and $\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text Q$ are irradiated by a monochromatic light source. The kinetic energy of an electron emitted from $\text P$ is $KE_\text P$ and that of an electron emitted from $\text Q$ is $KE_\text Q$.

**Given$\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text P>\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text Q$, which of these is possible?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

In photoelectric effect, the electrons are not all emitted with the same value of $KE$. For _any metal_, there is a range of kinetic energies that the electrons are emitted with.

##Solution

Since $\text P$ and $\text Q$ have different work functions, the range of $KE$ will be different for the two metals. 

**But**, the kinetic energy of the electrons emitted from the two metals depends on how tightly bound those particular electrons were before removal.
 Since an electron in $\text P$ can be more, less _or_ equally bound as an electron in $\text Q$, so **all** of the choices are possible.

Thus, the correct answer is 

$KE_\text P>KE_\text Q$, $KE_\text P<KE_\text Q$ and $KE_\text P=KE_\text Q$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two metal surfaces $\text P$ and $\text Q$ having work functions $\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text P$ and $\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text Q$ are irradiated by a monochromatic light source. The kinetic energy of an electron emitted from $\text P$ is $KE_\text P$ and that of an electron emitted from $\text Q$ is $KE_\text Q$.

**Given$\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text P<\left(\phi_{0}\right)_\text Q$, which of these is possible?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

In photoelectric effect, the electrons are not all emitted with the same value of $KE$. There is a range of kinetic energies that the electrons are emitted with for any metal.

##Solution

Since $\text P$ and $\text Q$ have different work functions, the range of $KE$ will be different for the two metals. 

**But**, the kinetic energy of the electrons emitted from the two metals depends on how tightly bound those particular electrons were before removal.
 Since an electron in $\text P$ can be more, less _or_ equally bound as an electron in $\text Q$, so **all** of the choices are possible.

Thus, the correct answer is 

$KE_\text P>KE_\text Q$, $KE_\text P<KE_\text Q$ and $KE_\text P=KE_\text Q$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A metal surface irradiated by light of a certain frequency undergoes photoelectric emission.

**Which of these best represents the average energy $\left( E_{\text{avg}}\right)$ spent by a photon in removing an electron from the metal surface?**$\\$
*Note: $\phi_0$ is the work function of the metal.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

$\phi_0$ is the _minimum_ energy that a photon needs to spend to remove an electron. We can say that $\phi_0$ removes only the most loose electron in the metal. Other electrons will be more tightly bound.

##Solution

The more tightly bound electrons will need more energy for being removed. So photons will need to spend energy greater than $\phi_0$ in removing such electrons. Thus, the average energy to be spent is greater than $\phi_0$.

The correct answer is:

$E_{\text{avg}}> \phi_0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A metal surface undergoes photoelectric emission when a light of certain frequency is incident on it.

**Which of these best represents the average energy $\left( E_{\text{avg}}\right)$ spent by a photon in removing an electron from the metal surface?**$\\$
*Note: $\phi_0$ is the work function of the metal.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

$\phi_0$ is the _minimum_ energy that a photon needs to spend to remove an electron. We can say that $\phi_0$ removes only the most loose electron in the metal. Other electrons will be more tightly bound.

##Solution

The more tightly bound electrons will need more energy for being removed. So photons will need to spend energy greater than $\phi_0$ in removing such electrons. Thus, the average energy to be spent is greater than $\phi_0$.

The correct answer is:

$E_{\text{avg}}> \phi_0$

assessment_items.question_content:
On shining monochromatic light on a metal surface, a large number of electrons are released. Consider the electron that has the maximum kinetic energy after being removed from the metal.

**Which of these best represents the portion of energy spent by a photon in removing this electron?**    
*Note: $\phi_0$ is the work function of the metal.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's say the energy of a photon is $E$. A part of this energy, $\phi$, is spent in removing the electron from the metal. The remaining appears as the kinetic energy $\left(KE\right)$ of the emitted electron.
$\\ \therefore E=\phi+KE$

##Solution

For the electron that has the maximum $KE$, $\phi$ should be minimum (since $E$ is constant). The minimum $\phi$ is what is known as the work function $\phi_0$. 

Thus, the photon spends $\phi_0$ to remove the electron that has the maximum kinetic energy.

The correct answer is:

$\phi_0$

assessment_items.question_content:
During photoelectric emission from a metal surface, a large number of electrons are released. Consider the fastest electron after being removed from the metal.

**Which of these best represents the portion of energy spent by a photon in removing this electron?**    
*Note: $\phi_0$ is the work function of the metal.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's say the energy of a photon is $E$. A part of this energy, $\phi$, is spent in removing the electron from the metal. The remaining appears as the kinetic energy $\left(KE\right)$ of the emitted electron.
$\\ \therefore E=\phi+KE$

##Solution

Since $E$ is constant, for the fastest electron (maximum $KE$), $\phi$ should be minimum. The minimum $\phi$ is what is known as the work function $\phi_0$. 

Thus, the photon spends $\phi_0$ to remove the electron that has the maximum kinetic energy.

The correct answer is:

$\phi_0$

assessment_items.question_content:
In a photoelectric effect experiment, the metal being used is replaced by another one having a _higher_ work function $\phi_0$.

**Which of these best represents the effect of this change on $K_{\text{max}}$, the kinetic energy of the most energetic electron?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

The electron under consideration is the most loosely bound electron. Since the photon spends minimum energy $\left( \phi_0 \right)$ for this electron's removal, the kinetic energy is maximum $\left( KE_{\text{max}} \right)$.
$\\ \therefore E=\phi_0+KE_{\text{max}}$

##Solution

The work function $\phi_0$ increases, so the photon has to spend more energy to remove the most loosely bound electron. Thus, a smaller portion of the photon's energy remains for the kinetic energy of the electron, i.e.,  $KE_{\text{max}}$ decreases.

The correct answer is:

$KE_{\text{max}}$ decreases.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a photoelectric effect experiment, the metal being used is replaced by another one having a _lower_ work function $\phi_0$.

**Which of these best represents the effect of this change on $K_{\text{max}}$, the kinetic energy of the most energetic electron?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

The electron under consideration is the most loosely bound electron. Since the photon spends minimum energy $\left( \phi_0 \right)$ for this electron's removal, the kinetic energy is maximum $\left( KE_{\text{max}} \right)$.
$\\ \therefore E=\phi_0+KE_{\text{max}}$

##Solution

The work function $\phi_0$ decreases, so the photon has to spend lesser energy to remove the most loosely bound electron. Thus, a larger portion of the photon's energy remains for the kinetic energy of the electron, i.e.,  $KE_{\text{max}}$ increases.

The correct answer is:

$KE_{\text{max}}$ increases.

assessment_items.question_content:
The energy of photons for a source of light is $E=4\ \text{eV}$. The work function of the metal on which the light is shining is $\phi_0=3\ \text{eV}$.

**Which of these is the most likely value of the kinetic energy of the slowest emitted electron?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

The more energy a photon spends in removing an electron, the lesser kinetic energy the electron comes out with. Different electrons on the surface of the metal need different energies for removal. Some maybe bound very tightly while others may be very loosely bound.

##Solution

When the photon spends its entire energy to remove an electron, then the electron is emitted with **zero** kinetic energy. This is the kinetic energy of the slowest emitted electron irrespective of the photon's energy and the work function of the metal.

The correct answer is:

$0 \ \text{eV}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The energy of photons for a source of light is $E=5\ \text{eV}$. The work function of the metal on which the light is shining is $\phi_0=2\ \text{eV}$.

**Which of these is the most likely value of the kinetic energy of the slowest emitted electron?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

The more energy a photon spends in removing an electron, the lesser kinetic energy the electron comes out with. Different electrons on the surface of the metal need different energies for removal. Some maybe bound very tightly while others may be very loosely bound.

##Solution

When the photon spends its entire energy to remove an electron, then the electron is emitted with **zero** kinetic energy. This is the kinetic energy of the slowest emitted electron irrespective of the photon's energy and the work function of the metal.

The correct answer is:

$0 \ \text{eV}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The energy of photons for a source of light is $E=5\ \text{eV}$. The work function of the metal on which the light is shining is $\phi_0=2\ \text{eV}$.

**Which of these best represents the range of kinetic energy of the emitted electrons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's say the energy of a photon is $E$. A part of this energy, $\phi$, is spent in removing the electron from the metal. The remaining appears as the kinetic energy $\left(KE\right)$ of the emitted electron. So,
$E=\phi+KE$.

##Solution

To emit the most energetic electron, the photon spends $\phi_0$ energy. So,

$\begin{align} 
KE_{\text{max}}&=E-\phi_0 \\\\
&=5\ \text{eV}-2\ \text{eV} \\\\
&=3\ \text{eV}
\end{align}$
To emit the slowest electron the photon spends its entire energy $\left(\phi=E\right)$. So the kinetic energy of the slowest electron $KE_{\text{min}}=0\ \text{eV}$.

The correct answer is:

$0\ \text{eV}\leq KE\leq3\ \text{eV}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The energy of photons for a source of light is $E=6\ \text{eV}$. The work function of the metal on which the light is shining is $\phi_0=4\ \text{eV}$.

**Which of these best represents the range of kinetic energy of the emitted electrons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's say the energy of a photon is $E$. A part of this energy, $\phi$, is spent in removing the electron from the metal. The remaining appears as the kinetic energy $\left(KE\right)$ of the emitted electron. So, 
$E=\phi+KE$.

##Solution

To emit the most energetic electron, the photon spends $\phi_0$ energy. So,

$\begin{align} 
KE_{\text{max}}&=E-\phi_0 \\\\
&=6\ \text{eV}-4\ \text{eV} \\\\
&=2\ \text{eV}
\end{align}$
To emit the slowest electron the photon spends its entire energy $\left(\phi=E\right)$. So the kinetic energy of the slowest electron $KE_{\text{min}}=0\ \text{eV}$.

The correct answer is:

$0\ \text{eV}\leq KE\leq2\ \text{eV}$
